1:
The witnesses take the gathering at Hebron as the long-delayed convergence of a whole nation upon the man God had chosen, and they read the elders' address along the three lines the text itself supplies. The grammatical-historical commentators set the scene and its scale. Keil and Delitzsch frame the chapter: after Ishbosheth's death David is anointed by all the tribes, storms the Jebusite fortress, and makes Jerusalem the capital that will soon become the religious centre of the nation. Charles Ellicott presses that 'all the tribes' means not merely the elders of v.3 but the great bodies of warriors numbered in 1 Chronicles 12:23–40, and he notes the striking detail that Judah, already David's own, joins the assembly, together with 4,600 Levites and Zadok among the priestly family. He dates the coming immediately after Ishbosheth's death, prepared for by Abner's earlier negotiations.

The reformed John Gill reads the tribes as sending ambassadors in their several names, once David had sufficiently cleared himself of any hand in the deaths of Abner and Ishbosheth — the very thing that had reconciled Israel to him. On the three reasons the elders give, the schools converge. Ellicott, the puritan Matthew Henry, and the wesleyan Adam Clarke lay them out alike: that they are David's own 'bone and flesh,' of a common descent that made separate nationhood unfitting (Genesis 29:14; Judges 9:2); that even under Saul it was David who 'leddest out and broughtest in Israel,' their proven captain; and that the LORD himself had appointed him to feed his people. Clarke states the logic bluntly — their own countryman, their tried general, and God's chosen king.

Henry turns the scene toward its pastoral and typical weight. The kinship plea becomes a lesson for the believer: those who take Christ for king may plead 'we are thy bone and thy flesh,' for he was made like his brethren in all things (Hebrews 2:17; compare Isaiah 3:6). David's former service teaches that he who is faithful in a little deserves to be trusted with more; and the divine word, 'thou shalt feed my people,' shows that princes are to feed and not fleece their subjects. Henry reads David's gradual rise — now anointed for the third time — as itself a figure of the Messiah's kingdom, which comes to its height by degrees, so that we do not yet see all things put under him (Hebrews 2:8) but shall (1 Corinthians 15:25). The one small crux is the source of the quoted promise: Ellicott observes that its exact words are nowhere on record, and takes it as a summary of God's communications to David, or the unrecorded speech of some prophet. The witnesses do not divide; together they see a nation at last owning the shepherd-king heaven had long since named.
2:
This verse gives the last two of the elders' three pleas, and the witnesses read in them the two offices David is called to hold together — captain and shepherd. The grammatical-historical commentators fix the words. The phrase 'thou wast he that leddest out and broughtest in Israel' is the old Hebrew idiom for the leader who commands his people both in war and in peace: even while Saul wore the crown, David was the true figure in the field, the man Israel followed to victory. Keil and Delitzsch attend to the divine sanction behind the human choice. When the Chronicler records that the tribes acted 'according to the word of the LORD through Samuel,' he shows that the secret anointing of the shepherd-boy (1 Samuel 16:1; 1 Samuel 16:12) now finds its complete and public fulfilment, Israel freely bringing to pass what God had long since decreed.

John Gill keeps the plain historical note that Saul had been king 'over us,' that is, over all the tribes, so that their present submission is the reversal of a long division. The weight of the verse, for the wesleyan John Wesley, lies in the word feed. 'Shalt feed' means to rule and care for them as a shepherd does his sheep (Psalms 78:70–71), and Wesley hears in it an admonition: David was not made king to advance his own glory but for the good of his people, whom he must rule with all tenderness and watch over with all diligence. The title of captain guards the same charge on its other side — the shepherd of Israel must also be exposed to the toils and perils of the camp for the flock's sake.

So the two titles the elders quote are not rivals but a single vocation. To 'lead out and bring in' is the captain's work; to 'feed' is the shepherd's; and Israel, remembering how David had already done the first under Saul, now trusts him with the second. Behind both stands the word once spoken to Samuel, which is why the people's decision is at bottom an act of obedience: they say at last what God had said from the first. The witnesses do not contend here. They agree that David's kingship is received, not seized — grounded in kinship, proven by service, and finally authorised by the LORD, who alone makes shepherds over his people and had marked out this one while he still kept sheep, so that 1 Samuel 18:16 (Israel loving David because he went out and came in before them) is now ratified by the whole nation.
3:
The elders' words become deeds, and the witnesses attend to the covenant and the anointing. John Gill reads the verse as the completion of the coming described earlier: the tribes act through their representatives, so that the elders who now assemble at Hebron are the very ones the several tribes had sent, meeting the king in a body once their favourable reception was reported. Keil and Delitzsch note that 'all the elders of Israel' simply defines the 'all the tribes' of v.1 more precisely, and that the covenant is struck 'before the LORD' — a solemn transaction under God's eye, as at the earlier league in 2 Samuel 3:21 — the Chronicler again adding that all was done according to the word of the LORD through Samuel.

The nature of that 'league' is where the schools shade apart. The grammatical-historical Ellicott warns against anachronism: it would be wrong to read here the establishment of a constitutional monarchy. Yet he allows that the covenant may have touched certain definite matters — David's undertaking to lead them against their enemies, to spare the remnant of Saul's house and its late adherents, and not to show partiality to his own tribe as Saul had done to Benjamin. The wesleyan John Wesley reads it as a mutual bond: David obliged himself to rule them according to God's laws, and the people promised him obedience. Between the two there is no real quarrel, only a difference of focus — Ellicott guarding the historical setting, Wesley drawing out the covenant's moral shape.

Clarke marks the number: this was the third time David was anointed, having now taken possession of the whole kingdom. The witnesses read the three anointings as the stages of a patient providence — the secret anointing by Samuel in Bethlehem (1 Samuel 16:13), the anointing by the men of Judah at Hebron (2 Samuel 2:4), and now the anointing by all Israel. David's advance to the throne is gradual by design, that his faith might be tried and his experience ripened, and that his kingdom might the more plainly typify the Messiah's, which comes to its height not at a stroke but by degrees. On the substance the company is agreed: a king received by covenant and confirmed by anointing, before the LORD, is a king who reigns as a trust and not as a spoil, bound to his people as they to him.
4:
The narrative pauses on a summary of David's age and reign, and the witnesses read the bare numbers for their chronology and their type. The grammatical-historical commentators do the arithmetic. Keil and Delitzsch note that the figures — thirty years old at his accession, forty years' reign — are given here at the outset but placed in the Chronicles at the close of David's life (1 Chronicles 29:26–27). Charles Ellicott draws the inferences: the statement shows about ten or twelve years have passed since the combat with Goliath, that the greater part of Saul's reign is treated very briefly in 1 Samuel, and that David was seventy years old at his death. The number is thus a quiet key to the whole span of David's life.

The reformed and puritan voices hear the age of thirty typologically. John Gill observes that thirty was the age of David's antitype, Christ, when he entered on his public ministry (Luke 3:23). Matthew Henry gathers the parallels: at thirty the Levites first entered their administration (Numbers 4:3), at that age the Son of David began his public ministry (Luke 3:23), and then men come to their full maturity of strength and judgment. The point is not idle numerology but a mark of fitness: David takes the throne at the age of settled manhood, as the greater Son of David would take up his ministry.

Beneath the figures lies the shape of a life that God had ripened slowly. The boy anointed in secret waited long years — hunted, exiled, king over Judah alone — before the crown of all Israel came to him at thirty; and it would rest on his head for forty years, the round number of a full generation and of testing. The witnesses do not debate here; they let the number teach its two lessons. To the historian it fixes David in time and harmonises the record with the Chronicles; to the reader of the whole Bible it points forward, for the shepherd who came to his kingdom by degrees, at the age of mature strength, prefigures the One whose kingdom likewise comes to its height by degrees and who entered his own public work at that same season of life.
5:
The summary continues with the division of David's forty years, and the witnesses read the split reign for its chronology and its meaning. The grammatical-historical commentators handle the fraction with care. Charles Ellicott observes that the 'six months' at Hebron, also recorded in 2 Samuel 2:11 and 1 Chronicles 3:4, is generally dropped from the round summaries (as in v.4) because it is only the fraction of a year; the sacred historians either omit such fractions or count them as whole years, which introduces the element of indefiniteness so marked in the parallel narratives of the kings of Israel and Judah. John Gill makes the same point from the other side: seven years and six months is how long the crown lingered in Saul's house after his death, so that David was near thirty-eight when the elders came to make him king.

Keil and Delitzsch take the verse as the hinge into the conquest of Zion. Because the reign 'over all Israel' and the reign 'in Jerusalem' are given as the same length, thirty-three years, the seizing of the citadel must have followed hard upon the anointing — David lost no time in making his capital. The two places, Hebron and Jerusalem, thus mark two eras of the reign, and the move between them is itself the great turn of the chapter.

Matthew Henry weighs the two cities. Hebron had its own fame — a priest's city, the place of Abraham's and the patriarchs' burial, called great in Joshua 14:15 — yet it lay too far south to be the heart of a united kingdom. Jerusalem was to be more: the holy city, the city of David, a name that runs famous to the very end of the Bible, where we read of a new Jerusalem (Revelation 21:2). Great kings, Henry notes, love to raise cities of their own; David raised none, but took one, and gave his name to a stronghold that would outlast every dynasty. The witnesses do not divide; they let the plain numbers carry both a historian's precision and a prophet's forward look, from the shepherd's long apprenticeship at Hebron to the throne on Zion that figures the everlasting kingdom.
6:
Here the witnesses turn from summary to siege, and they divide chiefly over one riddling boast — 'the blind and the lame.' The grammatical-historical commentators secure the ground and the grammar. Keil and Delitzsch and Charles Ellicott explain the Jebusites' confidence: their citadel stood on Mount Zion, shut in by deep valleys on three sides, so that in haughty security they could jeer that even the maimed would suffice to keep David out. Ellicott improves the rendering — 'Thou shalt not come hither; but the blind and the lame shall keep thee off' — and rehearses the city's tangled history: its king slain by Joshua (Joshua 10:23; Joshua 12:10), the town taken and burned by Judah (Judges 1:7), yet only partly held (Judges 1:21) and fallen again to the Jebusites (Judges 19:11), Jebus and Jerusalem being one city (1 Chronicles 11:4). John Gill adds why David waited: part of Jerusalem lay in Benjamin's lot, so he attempted nothing until all Israel, Benjamin included, had made him king.

The great division concerns who 'the blind and the lame' are. The wesleyan voices and Ellicott take them literally. John Wesley supposes the Jebusites, trusting fortifications so impregnable that invalids could man them, set a parcel of blind and lame folk on the wall in contempt of David; Adam Clarke, granting that scarcely a passage has more puzzled commentators, reads the same scene — maimed men posted to turn David's whole attempt into ridicule (and he notes Kennicott's proposed emendation of the verse). Against this stands the bolder reading of the puritan Matthew Henry, who offers two possibilities: either the Jebusites confided in their fortifications, setting invalids on the walls in scorn, or they confided in their gods — idol images which David in contempt calls 'the blind and the lame,' since they have eyes and see not, feet and walk not, brazen guardians they trusted as their strongholds (Daniel 11:38).

Beneath the philology the witnesses land on one lesson. Henry draws it: the enemies of God's people are often most confident, and most secure, when their day to fall draws near — the Jebusites could not believe an enemy would ever enter their gates (Lamentations 4:12; compare Jeremiah 37:10). Whether the taunt named cripples on the wall or gods that could not save, the boast was the swagger of a doomed pride, and the stronghold that seemed impregnable was already marked for David's hand.
7:
The boast of the Jebusites is answered in a single word — nevertheless — and the witnesses, though few here, make that word carry the weight of the verse. John Gill pictures Zion as a fortress standing apart from the lower town, exceedingly strong, so that its capture was both the key to the city and no easy prize; the taking of the citadel opened the way to all the rest, yet the taking itself was hard. The stress falls on the contrast: over against the sneer that the blind and the lame could hold the place, the text sets the flat fact that David took it.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch supply the name and the history. Zion — from a root meaning 'dry' or 'parched,' the arid hill — was the southern and loftiest height of Jerusalem, crowned by the citadel that alone had held out; for the lower, northern part of the city had already been taken by Judah and Benjamin shortly after Joshua's death (Judges 1:8). The clause 'the same is the city of David,' they note, is written by anticipation: David did not give the stronghold that name until he had chosen it for his residence and capital (v.9). The narrator, telling the story after the event, lets the future name slip in early, so that the reader sees at once what this rock is to become.

So the verse turns on the collision of a boast and a deed. The Jebusites had trusted the sheer sides of their hill and the pride of centuries; the parallel record in 1 Chronicles 11:4–9 tells the same conquest. What no tribe had wrested from them since the days of Joshua, David takes at the outset of his reign over all Israel, and the very strength that had made the fort a terror becomes, once taken, the safety of the king. The witnesses do not debate; they let the plain word stand. The 'dry hill' that seemed unassailable is carried, and by being carried is transfigured — the last Canaanite hold in the heart of the land becomes the city of David, the seat from which he will rule and toward which the whole Bible will look. The single word nevertheless marks the moment a pagan citadel passed over into the history of the people of God.
8:
This verse records David's battle-order in the moment of assault, and the witnesses labour over an obscure and much-transposed sentence. The grammatical-historical commentators lead. Keil and Delitzsch and Charles Ellicott agree that the word rendered 'gutter' (Hebrew tsinnor), found elsewhere only in Psalms 42:7, most probably means a watercourse or water-shaft connected with the precipices of Zion, and that the English clauses are needlessly transposed: with a slight change of the Masoretic vowels 'getteth up' becomes 'let him hurl,' so that the sense runs, 'Whosoever smites the Jebusites, let him hurl into the watercourses the lame and the blind.' David thus flings the enemy's own taunt back in their teeth, turning 'the blind and the lame' from their boast into their doom.

The phrase 'that are hated of David's soul' the witnesses read as the language of the ban: the Jebusites, as Canaanites, lay under the old sentence of destruction, and in this storming no quarter was to be given. John Wesley reads along the same grain — 'to the gutter' meaning whoever scales the fort to the top where the water-channel ran, and 'that are hated' meaning the Jebusites and the very cripples who had insulted David and blasphemed God. Both Wesley and Ellicott supply from 1 Chronicles 11:6 the reward-clause that the Hebrew here leaves out: 'Whosoever smiteth the Jebusites first shall be chief and captain.' John Gill simply anchors the day — 'on that day' is the day Zion fell.

Ellicott presses the supplement into a piece of the larger story. It was Joab who answered the challenge, went up first, and so won the command of all Israel's armies that he had hitherto held only in Judah — a fact, he observes, that helps explain the sense of obligation and restraint David afterward felt toward so dangerous a servant. And the closing words, 'Wherefore they said, The blind and the lame shall not come into the house,' Wesley and Ellicott take as the birth of a proverb: David may have ordained it to keep the memory of the exploit and of the Jebusites' insolence, so that the very phrase they had flung at him passed into a byword. The witnesses do not divide over doctrine here; their common work is to recover, from a broken text, a single vivid scene — the mocked king taking the unassailable rock, and the mockers' words rebounding upon their own heads.
9:
With the fortress won, David makes it his own, and the witnesses read the verse as the founding of a capital. John Gill marks the simple fact: David dwelt in the stronghold of Zion that he had taken, exchanging Hebron for the citadel he had just carried. Keil and Delitzsch draw out his reasons of state. In choosing the citadel for his palace and Jerusalem for his capital, David was guided both by the natural strength of Zion and by its situation — on the border of Benjamin and Judah, and near the centre of the land — so that the new seat belonged to no one tribe and stood open to all. The note that he 'built round about from Millo and inward' the Chronicler renders as building 'to the environs,' that is, an encircling wall over against the Millo.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott works on the topography. 'The fort' is the same word as the 'strong hold' of v.7; 'Millo,' always written with the definite article (except in Judges 9:6), is probably an old Canaanite name for the fortification on the northern end of Mount Zion, 'inward' from which the palace stood. Later kings saw its importance and strengthened it — Solomon (1 Kings 11:27) and Hezekiah (2 Chronicles 32:5) — while on every other side Zion was walled by its own precipitous ravines; though, he admits, the exact topography of ancient Jerusalem remains disputed.

The puritan Matthew Henry lifts the building work into figure. David dwelt in the fort whose strength had lately been his obstacle and was now his defence, and built houses round about for his attendants and guards, proceeding and prospering in all he set his hand to. Henry hears the gospel in it: the church is called Zion, the city of the living God; and as the Jebusites — Christ's enemies — with their blind and lame had first to be dispossessed before David could set up his throne, so the strong hold of the human heart must first be taken from its idols before the Son of David can reign there and make it his residence by the Spirit. The witnesses do not contend; they see one thing from three angles — a conquered rock becoming a king's house, a city's walls, and a standing type of the heart where Christ, having cast out its old masters, takes up his throne.
10:
This short verse states the secret of David's rise, and the witnesses, though few, will not let its brevity hide its weight. John Gill traces the outward increase: David 'went on, and grew great' in honour and wealth, in fame and reputation, subduing his enemies and enlarging his dominions. Keil and Delitzsch gloss it the same way — he increased in greatness, that is, in power and fame — but both fasten the growth to its cause, the clause that governs the whole: 'for the LORD God of hosts was with him.' The greatness is real, but it is derived; David rises because God is with him, not the reverse.

The title itself repays attention. 'The LORD God of hosts' — the God of armies — is the fitting name to stand at this point of the story, framed as it is by two wars with the Philistines; the King who grows great does so under the hand of the Lord of every host of heaven and earth. The puritan Matthew Henry draws the comfort the phrase holds: those who have the Lord of hosts for them need not fear what hosts of men or devils can do against them, and those who grow great must ascribe their advancement to the presence of God and give him the glory. Henry sets David against Saul, who had forfeited and lost that presence: the difference between the two reigns is not talent or fortune but this — that God was with the one and had departed from the other.

So the witnesses read the verse as both history and warning. As history, it summarises the steady, cumulative ascent of a reign that had begun in exile and now stood secure. As warning, it locates the whole of that success outside David himself: the moment greatness is traced to the king's own arm rather than to the God of hosts who was with him, it is misread. The sequel confirms it, for David himself will confess in v.12 that it was the LORD who established him. The company does not divide; they agree that the one thing needful in a king — as in any servant of God — is the abiding presence that Saul threw away and David kept, the presence that turns a hunted fugitive into an established and growing throne.
11:
The scene shifts from war to building, and the witnesses gather around the figure of Hiram of Tyre. The grammatical-historical commentators supply the man and his trade. Keil and Delitzsch and Charles Ellicott identify Hiram (also spelt Hirom and Huram) as the Tyrian king who was ally not only of David but afterward of Solomon, sending cedar and skilled builders for both palace and temple (1 Kings 5:1; 2 Chronicles 2:3) and joining Solomon in a merchant fleet. Ellicott raises the chronological knot: since this same Hiram was still living in Solomon's twenty-fourth year (1 Kings 9:10), either he reigned some fifty-seven years, or — more likely — his embassy to David came not at once but some years after the capture of Jerusalem, during the interval in which 'David went on and grew great' and so drew the notice of Tyre.

The identity of Hiram is the one real disagreement. John Gill holds that the Hiram here is the father of the Hiram who served Solomon, his earlier name Abibalus, Hiram becoming a common name of the kings of Tyre. Ellicott, Keil and Delitzsch, and the wesleyan Adam Clarke take him to be one and the same man across both reigns — Clarke adding that Hiram was a friendly prince and, no doubt, a believer in the true God, the helper of Solomon in building the temple. Ellicott notes only that Huram's own father was also named Huram (2 Chronicles 2:13), which may lie behind the confusion, and that the embassy must in any case fall late, since David was already settled in his 'house of cedar' before Solomon's birth (2 Samuel 7:2).

The puritan Matthew Henry draws the lesson from the workmen's hands. The Jews were husbandmen and shepherds, little given to architecture, so they leaned on the Phoenicians for their finest work; yet David's house, Henry says, was never the worse, nor the less fit to be dedicated to God, for being built by the sons of the stranger — a thing prophesied of the gospel church, that 'the sons of strangers shall build up thy walls, and their kings shall minister unto thee' (Isaiah 60:10). The witnesses do not finally divide except over Hiram's identity; together they read the Tyrian's cedar and craftsmen as a sign that David's throne is now owned and served by the nations round about, and that even the labour of outsiders is not too common to raise a house for the anointed of the LORD.
12:
The witnesses read this verse as David's own reading of his history — the moment the king interprets his success rightly. The grammatical-historical commentators note how he came to the knowledge. John Gill and Keil and Delitzsch agree that David 'perceived' it from the success of his enterprises: the steady prospering of all he set his hand to was itself the evidence that Jehovah had firmly established him and exalted his kingdom. Charles Ellicott presses the discernment behind the prosperity: David's greatness had not blinded him, for he saw that his blessings came to him as the head of the theocracy and for the sake of God's chosen people, not for his own aggrandisement.

That last clause — 'for his people Israel's sake' — the witnesses make the moral centre. The wesleyan John Wesley states it plainly: David was exalted that he might be a blessing to Israel and they be happy under his rule. Keil and Delitzsch ground it in election — God exalted the kingdom because he had chosen Israel as his people and promised to make it great. So the throne is a stewardship: the king is lifted up not for himself but for the flock, an echo of the shepherd-charge of v.2 now confessed by David himself.

The puritan Matthew Henry develops the double blessing of establishment and of perceiving it. David's kingdom was established, with nothing left to shake it — unlike Saul, made king but never established — because David typifies Christ, with whom God's hand is established and his covenant stands fast (Psalms 89:21). And David perceived it: many have the favour of God and do not discern it, and so want the comfort of it, but to be exalted and to know it — 'by this I know that thou favourest me' (Psalms 41:11) — is happiness indeed. Henry hears the promise that Christ shall be higher than the kings of the earth, highly exalted (Philippians 2:9), and yet insists on the servant-shape of all true rule, for kings are ministers of God to their people for good (Romans 13:4). The witnesses do not divide; they agree that David's greatness is a gift he neither seized nor misread — established by God, perceived by faith, and held in trust for the people's sake.
13:
On this verse the witnesses speak with an unusual unanimity of censure, for the multiplying of wives and concubines stands against the plain law of the king. The reformed John Gill says it flatly: it was not to David's honour, and it was contrary to the law of God (Deuteronomy 17:17), the concubines being a kind of secondary, half-wives. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch concede that a large harem was reckoned, from time immemorial, part of an oriental court, and judge that David let that custom seduce him into disregarding the prohibition — a fault for which he 'suffered many a heartburn afterwards,' not to speak of his great fall in the matter of Bathsheba (2 Samuel 11:2).

Charles Ellicott weighs the offence precisely: David came perilously near a violation of the law, though he did not, like Solomon after him, amass wives and concubines in enormous number for the mere magnificence of a great harem. He clears up the phrase 'out of Jerusalem' by the parallel 'at Jerusalem' (1 Chronicles 14:3), and notes that all told nineteen sons are named, the daughters passed over save Tamar, whose sorrow will fill a later chapter (2 Samuel 13:1). The wesleyan voices press the matter to principle. Adam Clarke observes that David had enough already, and that though the dispensation permitted polygamy, 'from the beginning it was not so'; since roughly fourteen males are born to thirteen females, polygamy is against nature and was never in God's original design. John Wesley reckons it among David's miscarriages — the multiplying of wives being expressly forbidden the king — and grants that it may have been policy, to enlarge his house and strengthen his interest by alliances; yet all this did not preserve him from coveting his neighbour's wife, but rather inclined him to it, 'for men who have once broke the fence will wander carelessly.'

The puritan Matthew Henry speaks for the whole company in his refusal to gloss the sin: 'Shall we praise him for this? We praise him not; we justify him not; nor can we scarcely excuse him.' The bad example of the patriarchs may have made David think there was no harm in it, and a full quiver is indeed a blessing; but one vine by the house, with God's blessing, may fill the land, while David's many wives did not keep him from the coveting and defiling of Bathsheba. Here the witnesses do not debate; from reformed, grammatical, wesleyan, and puritan they say one thing — that the king's disregard of God's law, however customary among kings, was the loosening of a fence through which greater ruin would afterward pour.
14:
The roll of David's Jerusalem-born sons begins, and the witnesses read the list less for its names than for the line that runs through it. John Gill notes only that these are the sons, the daughters unnamed, and that they seem to be the children of David's wives. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott does the harmonising work. The same list, with variations, stands in 1 Chronicles 3:5–8; the first four — Shammua, Shobab, Nathan, and Solomon — were, by that record, children of Bathsheba (Bath-shua), and so born later in David's reign. Though Solomon is placed last in every list, he was in fact the eldest of Bathsheba's surviving sons (2 Samuel 12:24), else he could hardly have been old enough to take the kingdom at his father's death; the variations among the lists, Ellicott adds, are chiefly differences of spelling.

The wesleyan Adam Clarke attends to the textual state of the list. The Hebrew names eleven children here, but the Septuagint no fewer than twenty-four, and Clarke rehearses the Greek roll with its evident corruptions — two Nathans, two Eliphalaths, two Napheks — a caution that the transmission of such name-lists is imperfect and that harmonising them requires care rather than force.

Yet the witnesses, having counted and compared, land on the two names that matter most for the whole Bible: Nathan and Solomon. Through these two sons of David the evangelists trace our Lord's descent — Solomon in the royal line of Matthew 1:6, Nathan in the line of Luke 3:31 — so that this domestic register in a conquered city is, unknown to the men who first read it, the seed-plot of the genealogy of Christ. The company does not divide here; the grammatical-historical labour of sorting spellings and sources serves a purpose deeper than antiquarian tidiness. From the household David gathered at Jerusalem, amid the very excess the previous verse lamented, God was quietly carrying forward the promise, so that out of this list of half-remembered princes would come both the throne of Solomon and, at the last, the Son of David in whom the kingdom stands for ever.
15:
The roll of David's Jerusalem-born sons runs on — Ibhar, Elishua, Nepheg, and Japhia — and here the material is thin, a single witness on a single name, so the synthesis must draw its weight from the verse's own words and its setting. John Gill offers the one comment: that the Elishua named here is called Elishama in the parallel record, a variation of spelling between the lists rather than a difference of person. It is a small note, but a characteristic one, and it opens onto the larger observation the grammatical-historical commentators had already made on the previous verse.

For Charles Ellicott had shown that the same catalogue, set down in 1 Chronicles 3:6 and again in 1 Chronicles 14:5, varies chiefly in spelling from copy to copy — Elishama there standing in one place where Elishua stands here, a copyist's slip that the lists themselves help to correct. Gill's note on this verse is simply one instance of that pattern: the sacred registers, transmitted by many hands, differ in the form of a name while agreeing in the substance, and the reader is meant to compare Scripture with Scripture rather than to stumble at a letter.

The verse's own words invite a quieter reflection. These four sons of David leave no further mark on the history: unlike Solomon, who would reign, or Nathan, through whom the line to Christ would run, or Absalom and Adonijah, whose ambitions would trouble the kingdom, Ibhar and Nepheg and Japhia and their brother pass out of the record as soon as they enter it, names only. Yet Scripture troubles to keep them, and to keep them accurately, comparing v.14 with the Chronicler's roll so that not one is lost. The witnesses, few and brief as they are here, thus teach by their very thinness: that the word of God preserves even the names history forgets, numbering the children of its king with a care that overlooks none, and that the fidelity of the record in so small a matter is a pledge of its fidelity in the great ones. The single note that survives — that one son bore two forms of his name across the parallel rolls — is itself a witness to that care, for a register kept loosely would not have troubled to be corrected, whereas Scripture sets one list beside another that the truth of even a name may stand. What looks at first like a bare fragment of a family tree becomes, on this reading, a quiet testimony to the exactness of the God who keeps the book.
16:
The list of David's sons closes with three names — Elishama, Eliada, and Eliphelet — and the witnesses spend their brief comment reconciling this roll with its parallels. John Gill observes that these are three more sons, by some other wife or wives, so that the tally here differs from the fuller record: where the Chronicler names nine at this point, the account before us gives fewer, because two of the Chronicler's names — a second Eliphelet and one called Nogah — are wanting. Following Kimchi, Gill suggests the reason: those two may have died without sons, and so are quietly passed over in a list meant to preserve the continuing house.

The grammatical-historical witness carries the same work forward. Set beside 1 Chronicles 3:6 and the total reckoned in 1 Chronicles 3:8, the names here are seen to be an abridgement rather than a contradiction — the two absent names deliberately omitted, most likely because their bearers left no posterity or died in infancy. The lists thus supplement one another: what one includes for completeness, the other drops for the sake of the living line, and the careful reader, comparing them, finds not error but two different purposes in the keeping of one family's record.

With this name the household of David is fully numbered, and the domestic peace of the chapter reaches its quiet height — the king established on Zion, his palace built by Tyrian hands, his sons born around him in the city of David. Yet the very next word will break the calm, for 'when the Philistines heard that they had anointed David king,' they came up to seek him (v.17). The witnesses do not debate over these three names; their labour is simply to show the record consistent with itself. But the placing of the list is not accidental. The settled house and the numbered sons stand as the token of a throne now firmly planted, and it is precisely this settled greatness — the anointing, the capital, the growing dynasty — that provokes the assault of the following verses, as though the powers ranged against the anointed of the LORD could not bear to see his kingdom at rest.
17:
The chapter turns back to war, and the witnesses read the Philistine assault as the predictable rage of a threatened foe. The reformed John Gill sets the motive: the Philistines had hoped Israel's long civil war would end in its ruin and had lain quiet; but now, seeing the nation united under David, strengthened, allied with Hiram, and master of Jerusalem, they judged it high time to check his greatness — and Gill passes on Kimchi's thought that it was at this juncture David penned the second Psalm, 'Why do the heathen rage' (Psalms 2:1). The wesleyan Adam Clarke reads the timing the same way: since Saul's fall the Philistines had held the principal places, and now that the whole nation had chosen David they struck before his army could grow and his kingdom settle.

The grammatical-historical commentators debate the geography. 'To seek David' means, for Keil and Delitzsch, to seek him out and smite him, and the phrase 'went down to the hold' becomes a puzzle: since David went down to it and then up from it to battle (v.19), the hold cannot be the lofty citadel of Zion. Keil and Delitzsch take it for the mountain stronghold in the desert of Judah where David had once sheltered from Saul (2 Samuel 23:14; 1 Chronicles 12:8); Charles Ellicott agrees it must be some stronghold near the Philistine army, but not the cave of Adullam; while the wesleyan John Wesley takes it for a fortified place, toward the Philistines, where David's people could rally and he could entrench his army. On the substance they agree — David withdrew to a defensible post before striking — and differ only on which post the text intends.

The puritan Matthew Henry lifts the campaign into figure. The very service for which David was raised up was to save Israel from the Philistines (2 Samuel 3:18), and Providence now gives him the occasion; in both battles the Philistines are the aggressors, pulling destruction on their own heads. Henry hears the pattern of the ages: the kingdom of the Messiah, as soon as it was set up, was thus vigorously attacked by the powers of darkness, Jews and Gentiles combined — 'the heathen raged, and the kings of the earth set themselves' — but in vain, for the ruin recoils upon Satan's own kingdom (Isaiah 8:9–10). The witnesses do not divide over the meaning of the war, only over the map: they agree that the newly anointed king is assailed at once, and that his safety lies, as the next verse will show, not in the hold he retreats to but in the LORD he consults.
18:
The verse fixes the enemy's position, and the witnesses read the valley of Rephaim for its name, its extent, and its menace. John Gill renders it 'the valley of the giants,' lying west of Jerusalem, and takes the Philistines' 'spreading themselves' there as a sign that they were very numerous. The grammatical-historical commentators fill in the ground. Charles Ellicott notes that the name is translated 'the valley of the giants' in Joshua 15:8, and describes a fruitful valley stretching some three miles south-west from Jerusalem, parted from the valley of Hinnom only by a narrow ridge — ample room for a large encampment. Both he and Keil and Delitzsch draw a chronological inference from the site: that the Philistines came here, close under the city, 'to seek David' proves that Jerusalem had already fallen into his hands, and Ellicott adds that they had encamped in this same valley in earlier days (2 Samuel 23:13).

The puritan Matthew Henry reads the spreading host as a design upon the capital. The Philistines pitched in Rephaim, hard by Jerusalem, hoping to master the city before David could finish fortifying it; and Henry observes that Jerusalem, from its infancy, has been aimed at and struck at with a particular enmity. Their very numbers, their spreading over the valley, made a formidable show — yet Henry turns the show against them: it is with the church's enemies as with those who 'go up on the breadth of the earth' (Revelation 20:9), for the further they spread themselves, the fairer mark they make for God's arrows.

So the witnesses let the geography carry both a fact and a promise. The fact is that David is beset at the very moment of his triumph, the giants' valley filled with an army pressing toward his new-won city; the placing of the enemy so near proves how much was at stake and how little time the king had won before the trial came. The promise is the one Henry draws from the enemy's own confidence: the wide array that looks so terrible is, to the eye of faith, only a larger target for the God who fights for his anointed. The company does not debate the verse; they agree that the valley of the giants, ancient haunt of Israel's oldest foes, becomes the appointed stage on which the LORD will break the Philistine power, and that the numberless host spread across it is gathered less for David's ruin than for its own.
19:
Before he strikes, David asks; and the witnesses make the verse a lesson in the piety that will not move without God. John Gill names the means: David inquired by Abiathar the priest, through the Urim and Thummim in the ephod he wore. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch confirm it — David sought by the holy lot whether he should go out and whether God would give the enemy into his hand — and observe that the twofold inquiry here and again in v.23 overturns the claim that the sacred lot fell out of use after Saul's death; the newly anointed king leans on it still.

The wesleyan Adam Clarke draws the spirit of it: David 'considered himself only the captain of the LORD's host, and therefore would not strike a stroke without the command of his Superior.' The king who had every worldly reason for confidence — a great army, a fresh throne, a provoking foe — will not presume upon it, but holds his hand until heaven speaks.

The puritan Matthew Henry unfolds the double question David asks. His conscience frames the first — 'Shall I go up?' — the question of duty; for though the answer might seem plain (what was he made king for but to fight the LORD's battles?), a good man loves to see God going before him in every step, and besides, some of these Philistines, like Achish, had once sheltered David in his distress, so that he will not lift a sword against them without warrant. His prudence frames the second — 'Wilt thou deliver them into mine hand?' — the question of success, by which he owns that he can conquer only as God gives the victory, and refers the whole cause to God's good pleasure. To both the answer comes full and free: 'Go up; for I will doubtless deliver the Philistines into thine hand.' Henry hears in that 'doubtless' the same assurance given to every soldier of Christ, that the God of peace will bruise Satan under our feet shortly (Romans 16:20), so that we do not fight at uncertainty. The witnesses do not divide; they agree that David, with force enough in hand, relied more on the promise than on the army, and that the pattern of his warfare — inquire, then obey — is the pattern of all victory that lasts.
20:
The battle is won, and the witnesses hear in the new place-name David's confession that the LORD, not the king, broke the enemy. The grammatical-historical commentators fix the name and its sense. Charles Ellicott and Keil and Delitzsch read Baal-perazim as 'lord' or 'possessor of breaches,' and picture David's onset as a sudden bursting, like the breaking-out of pent waters, that carried all before it; the victory was so signal that it gave the spot a lasting name and was remembered centuries later as a byword for divine aid (Isaiah 28:21). Ellicott is careful to add that the name has no reference to the heathen god Baal, but uses the word only in its plain sense of 'lord.'

The wesleyan voices press the confession the name enshrines. Adam Clarke observes that David 'very properly attributes the victory to Jehovah, without whose strength and counsel he could have done nothing,' and reads Baal-perazim as commemorating the LORD's own interference. John Wesley renders it 'the master of the breaches,' so that in the very naming David 'ascribes all to God,' and glosses the figure 'as the breach of waters' by floods that burst their banks and overturn whatever stands in their way.

The puritan Matthew Henry gives the confession its fullest voice. David smote the Philistines by dint of sword, and having done it gave his God the glory: 'The LORD hath broken forth upon mine enemies before me — I could not have done it if he had not done it before me.' God opened the breach as one opens a gap in a dam, which, once opened, only widens; the chief part of the work was God's doing, so that 'not unto us, but unto the LORD, give glory' (Psalms 115:1). Henry hears in the burst waters the hope of a final desolation upon the enemy, the opening of the sluice; and he reads the naming of the place as David's care that posterity should take notice of it to God's honour. The witnesses do not divide; from grammatical, wesleyan, and puritan they agree in one thing — that the victory's monument is not a trophy to David but a name that hands the praise to God, teaching every later reader that where the breach is made, it is the LORD who breaks forth.
21:
The fleeing Philistines leave their gods behind, and the witnesses read the abandoned images as a parable of the impotence of idols. John Gill explains why the images were there at all: the Philistines had brought their idol-gods to battle to protect them and give success, perhaps in imitation of Israel, who had once carried the ark into the camp against them (1 Samuel 4:10–11); it was, he notes, the custom of many nations to take their gods to war. Adam Clarke says the same, recalling that Israel's own experiment with the ark ended in its loss. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott adds the textual point: the suddenness and completeness of the rout is shown by their leaving the images on the field, and the statement that David 'burned' them is drawn from the Chronicler (1 Chronicles 14:12), the Hebrew here saying only that he 'took them away' — David disposing of them, as the law required, by fire (Deuteronomy 7:5).

The puritan Matthew Henry draws the scene vividly. The Philistines had borne their images as protectors, in mimicry of the ark, but once put to flight they could not stay to carry them off — the idols a mere burden to weary beasts (Isaiah 46:1) — and so left them to fall with the rest of the baggage into the conqueror's hands. Their gods failed them; God can make men weary of what they doted on, and compel them to cast their idols 'to the moles and the bats' (Isaiah 2:20). David turned the other plunder to use but burned the images, as God had appointed.

The sharpest point the witnesses save for last, and here John Wesley and Henry meet in a single irony, which Henry credits to Bishop Patrick: when the ark of the true God fell into Philistine hands, it consumed them — Dagon broken on his threshold, the cities plagued — but when these images fell into the hands of Israel, 'they could not save themselves from being consumed.' The contrast is the whole lesson. The living God, even captive, overthrows his captors; the dead gods, even defended, cannot rescue their own bodies from the flames. The company does not debate; they agree that the field strewn with abandoned idols is Scripture's own commentary on the vanity of the nations' gods, and that the fire David kindles over them is an act of obedience and a sermon at once.
22:
The beaten enemy returns, and the witnesses read the second coming-up as a study in the obstinacy of a hardened foe. John Gill notes the renewed assault, adding from Josephus that the Philistines came now with an army three times larger than before — defeat had not cured them but only stung them to a greater effort. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch and Charles Ellicott read the return in the same key: though thoroughly beaten, the Philistines soon reappeared to repair their loss, and finding that David had not followed up his first victory — perhaps because he was not yet sufficiently prepared — they gathered again in the very valley where they had just been broken.

That detail, that they mustered a second time in Rephaim, the witnesses do not pass over lightly. It is the mark of a foe who will not learn: the ground of one humiliation becomes the chosen field of the next attempt, as though the memory of Baal-perazim could be erased by pitching camp on the same plain. The puritan Matthew Henry gives the theological reading — in the latter battle the Philistines came up again to recover what they had lost, 'their hearts being hardened to their destruction.' The renewed war is thus not merely a second danger but a second judgment, the enemy pressing forward to the very ruin they might have fled.

The wesleyan John Wesley fastens on the word 'spread themselves,' which, he says, intimates that they were very numerous and made a formidable appearance — so the church's enemies are seen going up 'on the breadth of the earth' (Revelation 20:9). Yet Wesley turns the very breadth of the array into their undoing: the wider they spread themselves, the fairer a mark they make for God's arrows. The witnesses do not divide here; they agree that the second Philistine campaign, larger and no wiser than the first, shows how a defeated hostility, refusing to own the hand that struck it, only multiplies its forces for a heavier fall — and that the enlarged host filling the giants' valley is gathered, like the first, for a judgment it is too hardened to escape.
23:
Faced with the enemy a second time, David asks a second time, and the witnesses note both his renewed dependence and the changed answer he receives. John Gill marks the piety: though David had prevailed before and might have presumed on the precedent, he would not engage again without the mind and will of God, knowing that victory hangs on him alone. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch report the oracle's new instruction — not to go up and attack in front as before, but to wheel round behind the foe and come upon them 'over against the baca-shrubs' — and pause over the rare word: found only here and in 1 Chronicles 14:14, rendered pear-trees by the Septuagint and mulberry by the Rabbins, both uncertain, the baca being a balsam-like shrub whose weeping sap may have given it its name. Charles Ellicott supplies the tactic's logic: the enemy, on the same field, would look for an attack from the same quarter as before, so David is sent to circle round and fall on them unexpectedly from the opposite side.

The wesleyan voices draw the practical lesson from the divine strategy. Adam Clarke observes that though the LORD has promised the victory, he bids David 'fetch a compass' and use means, 'for God will not work without using human means,' and so teaches his servant caution, prudence, and dependence on divine strength together. John Wesley reads the command exactly — 'go up' meaning directly against them, which David is forbidden, and 'behind' meaning where they least expect him — and states the balance in a sentence: 'God's purposes and promises do not exclude men's endeavours.'

The puritan Matthew Henry hears in the altered order a token of God's nearer presence. God bids David not fall on directly as before but draw back and circle round, appointing him to stand still, in a manner, and see the salvation of the LORD (Exodus 14:13). The witnesses do not divide; their common note is that God does not repeat himself. The first victory came by frontal assault and by David's sword; the second is to come by a flanking march and, as the next verse will show, by the LORD going out before him — so that even in triumph David is kept from the presumption of method, taught afresh to inquire, and shown that the same God who gives the promise appoints the means and varies them at his pleasure.
24:
The oracle gives David a sign to wait for, and the witnesses divide gently over its nature while agreeing on its lesson. Some hear a supernatural march. John Gill reports that the rustling in the treetops was as the going of men or of armies moving in the air, and passes on the reading of Jarchi and Rabbi Isaiah, that it signified angels sent of God, moving at that moment to help David and destroy the Philistines. The puritan Matthew Henry takes the same high view: God promised to charge the enemy himself by an invisible host of angels, 'the march of an army in the air upon the tops of the mulberry trees' — for angels tread light, and he who walks upon the clouds can walk upon the tops of trees.

Others hear a timed and natural sound. The wesleyan Adam Clarke judges that probably only a rustling among the leaves is meant (the Targum says 'a noise,' the Arabic 'the sound of horses' hoofs'), and adds a shrewd reason for the sign: had there been no evident supernatural interference, David might have credited his own clever manoeuvre for the victory, so God gives a token that the triumph is his. John Wesley reads it as a noise, which God would cause, serving a double end — to assure David that the LORD was coming to help, and to affright the Philistines, who would not perceive David's army until he was upon them. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch and Charles Ellicott stand between: 'the sound of a going' is the advance of an army, the sign of an army of God — like the visions granted to Jacob (Genesis 32:2–3) and Elisha (2 Kings 6:17) — and the same word is used of the march of the hosts of the LORD (Judges 5:4; Psalms 68:7); 'bestir thyself' means, literally, be sharp — act quickly.

Whether angels' tread or timed wind, the witnesses agree on the point, and Henry states it: 'the LORD go out before thee' does not excuse David from going out too. Though God promised to smite the host, David must bestir himself and pursue when he heard the sound, for God's grace must quicken our endeavours (Philippians 2:12–13). Henry hears the deepest echo of all — the Spirit who came upon the apostles 'with a sound from heaven as of a rushing mighty wind' (Acts 2:2), at which they too must rise and go forth. The company does not finally divide: the sign in the treetops, however explained, is God's own signal, teaching that the victory is his and yet that the servant must move the moment he hears his Lord move.
25:
David obeys to the letter, and the witnesses read the closing verse for its obedience, its geography, and its type. John Gill fixes the note that governs all: 'David did so as the LORD commanded him' — in all things obedient, where Saul was not; he circled behind the host as directed, waited for the sound in the treetops, and then rose and fell upon the enemy. The whole victory hangs on that exact compliance, the king moving neither before nor beyond his orders.

The grammatical-historical commentators work on the place-names of the pursuit. David smote the Philistines 'from Geba until thou come to Gazer'; but Keil and Delitzsch and Charles Ellicott agree that 'Geba' is a scribal slip for 'Gibeon,' the reading of 1 Chronicles 14:16, since Geba lay north-east of Jerusalem (Joshua 18:24) and quite off the line, whereas Gibeon, to the north-west (Joshua 9:3), lies on the way to Gezer at the edge of the Philistine plain. Ellicott measures the chase — some twelve miles from Gibeon, and more before it — and explains the north-westerly flight by David's flanking march from the south; he adds that the Chronicler places these two battles between the failed and the successful attempts to bring up the ark, and that the exact chronology can no longer be recovered.

The wesleyan Adam Clarke turns the perfect obedience into a searching meditation: David punctually obeyed, and all succeeded — so why are such supernatural directions not given now? Because, Clarke answers, they are not asked for; and not asked because not expected; and not expected because men have not faith, being sunk in a refined atheism that grants God no real concern with his creatures. He recalls, too, that David was appointed to feed the people: once the keeper of a flock, he is now the shepherd of Israel, meant to guide and protect and not to oppress — for the oldest and best name of a king is 'shepherd of the people.' The puritan Matthew Henry completes the type. David waited till God moved and stirred only then, trained up in dependence on providence; and so, when the Messiah's kingdom was to be set up, the apostles who were to beat down the devil's kingdom were charged to attempt nothing till they received the promise of the Spirit, who came with a sound from heaven, and then went forth 'conquering, and to conquer' (Revelation 6:2). The witnesses do not divide; they end the chapter on one note — that the victory belongs to the God who commands, and the honour to the servant who obeys, waits, and then strikes.
